test: PPP half-life ANNUAL, with real Hong Kong CPI (de-tautologized), apples-to-apples
why: the earlier HKD result used nominal rate only (no HK CPI) — potentially a peg tautology. This adds real HK inflation to test if the anchor stabilizes the REAL rate.
headline: Adding real HK CPI OVERTURNS the earlier HKD result. HKD real rate is NO LONGER clearly stationary.
interpretation: Hong Kong's currency board fixes the NOMINAL rate, but HK ran large inflation differentials vs the US (double-digit in the 1980s-90s), so its REAL exchange rate drifted like a floater's. A nominal peg did NOT deliver a stable REAL exchange rate. HKD's real-rate half-life (3.5yr) and variance (0.051) are only modestly better than floaters and overlap them (CAD 4.4yr).
what_this_means_for_CS: This CUTS AGAINST the simple CS FX-stability story. Even a HARD nominal anchor (HKD board) failed to stabilize the real exchange rate once real inflation is included. A CS currency anchors INFLATION (not the nominal rate) — which is arguably BETTER positioned for real-rate stability than HKD (since it targets the very thing — inflation differentials — that wrecked HKD's real rate). BUT that is a HOPEFUL INFERENCE, not shown: no CS-style currency exists to test, and this result shows the anchored analogue we DID have does not exhibit the claimed real-rate stability.
verdict: HONEST NET on the FX interface: (1) monthly variance decomposition FAILED falsification; (2) volatility gap ~2x but confounded; (3) PPP half-life: floaters show the puzzle (robust), and the one hard-anchor analogue (HKD) does NOT show real-rate stability once its CPI is included. The CS FX-stability claim is NOT empirically supported by the available analogues. The strongest honest statement is a NULL/UNPROVEN, possibly with a theoretical argument that CS's inflation-anchor targets exactly what broke HKD — but that argument is untested.
audio_implication: Audio international segment MUST stay 'under-specified, say so honestly.' If anything this STRENGTHENS the case for NOT claiming FX stability — the analogue evidence is null-to-negative, not supportive.
